chapters.title: Miles Kelly
chapters.text:
Miles Kelly is not one of a kind. Not in this place. This is SunCity, where killers,
deviants, and those whose actions are beyond most human comprehension are sent to spend the
remainder of their days. Walking into Receiving and Discharging on his first day in prison, Miles
felt very nervous. He was an 18-year-old first-time offender carrying a 50-year sentence on his
shoulder. An officer called names from a piece of paper that she held in her hands. She called,
“Miles!” and he hastily stood on the wall just as the guys before him had been instructed to do.
His heart began to pick up its pace as he stood next to two older criminals who were having a
discussion about all the prisons they’ve been to and all the violent acts they’d participated in and
witnessed. Hearing these gruesome stories only made him wearier. His blood pressure spiked
significantly. Still, he kept a stern face while trying to hide how he really felt. He was petrified!
‘I am not supposed to be here.’ Miles thought to himself. “Miles!” Someone yelled. He quickly
snapped out of his dazed and scared thoughts before asking, “Did you call Miles?” “About three
times! When someone calls your name, I suggest you answer, kid.” The officer pointed to a door
that was slightly open. Entering the room, there were two officers sitting at a table covered with
brown folders. They looked overworked. One of the officers looked up from whatever he was
reading before telling Miles with a stern voice to shut the damn door and have a seat. “It seems
that you’ll be spending the rest of your days with us. They gave you a fifty-year sentence? Is that
right, Mr Kelly, is it?” The officer asked. “Yeah, they gave me fifty years. I am still trying my

best to come to grips with all that time.” Miles answered. “Well, I have a few questions I want to
ask you before we place you into General Population, OK?” Miles shrugged and shook his head
up and down indicating that he understood the officer. “Is there any reason that you cannot be
placed in General Population? Do you have any enemies? Have you cooperated with law
enforcement? Are you scared to go into General Population?” At first Miles did not know what
to say. He took a minute to really think about his answer. The officer saw how hesitant he was
and reacted. “Answer me boy!” He said to Miles. Miles shook his head no to all these questions,
but in all honesty, he was scared enough that he contemplated lying for a second. “We are going
to have to hear you verbally say no to the questions, young man.” The officer said. A sharp
“No.” slipped off Miles’ tongue before he had the chance to really think about his safety. All the
colour drained from his face as he realised, he had just placed himself in a den full of lions. “You
know it’s rough out there. There are inmates walking around with hidden shivs, ready to stab you
to death.” The fat officer went on to say. “Are you prepared for that?” Miles’ hands tightened
around the armrest. The officer’s words had really gotten to him. “All right, you can leave now.”
The bony-faced officer said. Miles got up and made his way to the door that would place him in
another world. He now shared a space with rapists and murders. All kinds of deviants and not
one could be trusted. His first night in there was really rough. The bed was uncomfortable and
his roommate snored like a lion’s roar. The toilet smelled, the floor was sticky and the air was
musty. It was truly hell in there. This was only his first night and already he wanted out. Miles is
convicted of first-degree murder and kidnapping charges. Some say he got off easy on his
sentence. Throughout his entire trial, Miles maintained his innocence. ‘I was framed.’ He liked to
repeat this to anyone who would listen. But he is not one of a kind. Ninety Percent of everyone
in SunCity claims the same thing. All the guilty men, are all innocent. Miles could be a true

deviant. He could be one of the worst ones in SunCity. Beneath his soft exterior and easy-going
persona of a shy young teen, he is a monster. A psychopath who has murdered innocent people
without a second's thought. “You killed a girl, didn’t you?” He heard one of the inmates say.
Before, he’d see the look in people’s eyes when they realise that they have a monster under their
bed. It was fear, a combination of disbelief and horror. This was exactly what Miles was used to.
What scared him though, what made him feel so uneasy about being locked up, was what he did
not see. He did not see that they were afraid. He did not see the glint in the inmate’s eye that told
him that yes, he was afraid. He never noticed it. Miles always had been able to disappear into the
crowd. When he finally showed people who he truly was, he would make them very afraid.
Inside SunCity though, no one was afraid of him and although he had done some of the most
awful things, his demeanour was non-threatening. “You killed her, didn’t you? Is that why you
are in here with killers? You killed some chick?” The inmate prodded him. Miles admitted to the
inmate that sure indeed he had done it. He had committed the gruesome crime which he was
incarcerated for. But this was only a calculated move on his part. He needed the other inmates to
be afraid of him, so he began to saw the seed of his legend, to make the other deviants afraid of
him. It was a cruel thing to do, but a necessary one. His plan became clear, the inmates would
begin to fear him and that should eventually lead to his rise within the prison. He decided to find
a gang inside so he would be protected. Prison gangs all come in different colours and shapes.
They are white, dark skinned, Asian, Indians and some made up entirely of guys that have red
hair. Miles is only unique in the way he looks. His hair is jet black and straight, it flows down the
middle of his back in long waves that hang past the small of his back, falling down towards the
bottom of his spine. His skin is pale white and almost flawless. His face is clean-shaven and has
deep set blue eyes and light pink lips. His features are so delicate, so exquisite that every man in

prison wants a piece of this gorgeous angelic child that is currently in their grasp. Miles, being
the polite person he is, does not notice any of these things about himself.
“That guy is crazy, he told me he killed like ten guys.” Says Loki to another inmate.
Loki is Miles’ cell mate. He’s 5’11 standing straight and an inch off when he slumps his back as
he always does. He is a black male from somewhere rough he does not like to talk about. Exactly
5 years ago, Loki was caught scamming everybody out of their hard earned. That is why he is
here; he is in a sense a common thief. Aside from assaulting his cellmate Miles with his
uproarious snores late at night, he has been a rather delightful bunk-mate. He would sit there and
listen to Miles as he went on and on about his exploits, keeping in mind not to interrupt him as
he speaks. Miles really appreciated this. When the prisoners got out of their cells for the day,
Loki would go on and tell anyone who would listen about his sociopath of a cellmate. These
stories were shared all throughout the prison and soon enough every man had heard at-least three
times about the time Miles gutted a woman with a blunt knife and then preceded to dump her
body in a forest. Miles became infamous. But not everyone believed the stories. There were
those who believed that Loki was lying. Others believed he was just exaggerating.
When the last convict left the dining area of General Population, Miles walked to the
cafeteria and grabbed a bowl of soup. He sat down on a chair and picked up the spoon to shovel
a large amount of the hot liquid into his mouth. The soup tastes nothing like any food he’s ever
tasted before. The flavour comes together as a mix of tomatoes, garlic, salt, pepper and spices.
The taste reminds him of someone’s home cooking. The smell of chicken wings fills his nose
again, making him hungry for more. As he finishes the food, he begins to contemplate if he
should eat lunch at the mess hall or return to his cell. The question goes from one ear and out the
other as a loud banging noise catches his attention. He turns around to find the source of the

noise and sees that the other inmates seem to think something was wrong. All of the guards turn
to the window, where a massive explosion could be seen blowing the glass to pieces. Miles
watches as the guard that was in front of him starts moving towards the broken window.
Suddenly a group of armed men come crashing through the exploded area. They begin shooting
almost randomly but with intent. Their intention was to wound, not kill the guards who would
stand in the way of their objectives, whatever they were. “Everybody on the ground! This is a
hostage situation. Do whatever we say and hopefully you will get out of this alive.” ~ a gun
toting mercenary shouts this at everybody within ear shot. They all comply. All the grizzled
inmates lay on the cold hardened floor in fear. They were all helpless. Some of the guards were
wounded badly and they too surrendered and were on the floor with the rest of the prisoners. Out
of the wreckage, a woman appears. She looks to be in her early thirties. She walks over to the
prisoner closest to the wall. She kneels down beside him and pokes him in the stomach with a
long syringe. The inmate’s eye turns white as he falls unconscious, seemingly dead. The rest of
the inmates watch with open mouths and wide eyes, in total disbelief at what they were seeing.
Nobody says a word. They’re afraid to even breathe. After a minute, a mercenary takes the
needle out of the passed-out man and gives it to another guard. The same process is repeated
with each of the bodies that lay before them. Each of the corpses gets a dose of a sedative to
make them sleep. When the mercenaries are finished injecting everyone, they start to walk away
but a voice calls out to them from behind. “Wait!” The mercs turn around to find a very
distraught looking young man standing with an injured leg.
“What do you want?” It is Miles, somehow after being given the sedative he is still
standing. “I said what do you want dammit?” He asks again. The woman looks strangely at him
as if gazing into his very soul. “We want you.” For a second there is a look of disbelief on Miles’

face and he falls to the ground. Finally, the sedative had taken effect. The mercs begin loading
some of the unconscious prisoners onto a bus one by one. They are going off some list, picking
exactly who they needed from the decay of prisoners. “Him too.” Says the woman pointing at an
unconscious Miles. “But ma’am, he is not on the list.” A merc responds. The woman appears
annoyed and exclaims, “Just do what I say.” before heading into a different part of the prison. It
was quiet. Very quiet, eerily so. The halls of SunCity Prison were dark, gloomy and dreary,
almost like a tomb. No alarms went off. The mercs had some sort of jamming device which
prevented any alarms from going off the moment they entered the building. The woman walks
into an office and there she contends with some guards who were still standing. They open fire.
She manages to take cover and shoot back, hitting one of them on the leg. At this point the only
guards still fighting are one man with a shot out leg, his fat partner and another guard who did
not carry a weapon. The shooting goes on for a while until the guard runs out of bullets, which
the woman notices. “Give up now and I won’t kill you.” she says. Her conviction was well
enough because as she made that offer, the remaining guards all scurried away. The woman then
enters an office and proceeds to ransack the place clearly looking for something. After searching
for a while, she finds a small safe that might house what she requires when suddenly the alarms
start ringing and then she knows she has to get out before reinforcements come. With the help of
the mercs, they carry the safe back to the bus and drive off. They leave with 26 inmates, gagged,
tied and drugged with some sort of sleeping agent. The bus drives far from the prison until it
reaches a dirt road which leads into some mountains. They stop at an old abandoned mining
camp. As Miles wakes, he can hardly believe the sight before him. The mine shaft is empty and
dusty. The mines are full of debris and there are rats roaming everywhere. There are no people
here, nobody working on anything, nobody living. It is like a ghost town. The bus makes its way

towards the entrance of the cave and they exit to find a truck waiting for them. “Miss Avery, you
have a call, it’s him.” Says a Merc. The woman who led this whole prisoner heist kidnapping
mission is named Avery Melblac. She takes the phone and speaks. “Hello…?”
Miles fully wakes up. “What happened? Where am I? How did I get here?” He sits up on
a cot and rubs the sleep out of his eye. He can hear muffled voices coming from outside his door.
They sound suspiciously familiar but he cannot put his finger on who they belong to. He listens
to hear if there is someone else in the room with him. The voices remain quiet, so he decides to
investigate further. He slips off his cot and quietly tip toes his way to the door. Just as he reaches
out for the handle, his hand touches air. There is no door. Miles looks around to find there is no
door anywhere, just darkness. He looks around frantically until his eyes landed on a switch near
him. There was a little click followed by a low glow of soft green lights and the sound of doors
creaking open. Slowly, Miles made his way to one of the rooms and peaked inside. He saw a
huge facility filled with many people in cages. Most of the people seemed to be crying, others
shouting at the top of their lungs. It was horrible. “What did they give us?!” A man yelled loudly.
“They gave us a lot of things.” Replies a female in the cage next to him. “What was it?” Asks the
man. They had given them just enough to knock them out for an extended period of time without
killing them. Miles stood there calculating and studying his surroundings. No doubt about it,
these people were being held against their will. And judging from the screams and cries coming
from the other cells, it appears that they were being tortured or abused. A man with a gun
grabbed Miles and out put him inside one of these cages. The hostages were kept locked up for
God knows how long. Miles at some point wanted to help these poor bastards and help himself if
he could. But right now, there was no way he could get free. He began to rethink his plan to
escape. A risky one he admits to himself. After about two hours of listening to the screaming and

yelling of those in the next cages, Miles felt tired and decided to rest. He laid down on his cot
and started to fall asleep until something caught his eye. A flash of red. On the bottom corner of
his cell. Miles carefully got on his hands and knees and peered over the edge of the metal grate.
There was blood. Blood of a person’s body lying beneath him. He felt that he should not look but
curiosity got the better of him. In the dim light of the cell, Miles saw that the blood was pouring
from a wound on somebody’s chest. Somebody who was no longer moving. His head turned
slightly to check on the woman in the cage next to him. She was awake. She looked at him in
fear, “Oh my God. Did you see? That man just killed himself just like that.” She says, tears
welling up her eyes as the realization of what she had witnessed hit her. Miles did not know what
to tell her, he did not know what to do. She just saw a man dying and understandably she could
not keep herself together. As he was about to calm her down, Avery Melblac walked on over to
the cells where the hostages were being held and got on a PA system and begun to make an
announcement. “Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to Defiance…

chapters.title: Jonathan Reed
chapters.text:
Joseph Conrad said, ‘The belief in a supernatural source of evil is not necessary. Men
alone are quite capable of every wickedness.’ He was wrong. Growing up, Jonathan’s father had
always been obsessed with criminal behaviour. He studied it for years. His wife had been
murdered and so he devoted all his time trying to understand why it happened. The idea of evil
being real was one that his dad took quite seriously and Jonathan grew up believing in it as well.
They both came to the conclusion that evil acts must be a direct result of some unknowable
supernatural force outside the control of all men. If this were the case, they believed that people
are no more responsible for this condition than being born with cystic fibrosis or something
along those lines. But what about sociopathy or psychopathy. Those terms are uncertain and
subjective as evil is and in one sense, sociopathy and evil could be opposites. When someone is
said to be a sociopath, it explains their actions, if not justifying them. As a way to punish this we
might recommend hospitalization or imprisonment for a sociopath who committed terrible
crimes. Evil does not get therapy or rehabilitation—it gets locked away in the far depths of hell
and for as long as possible. To the extent that we cannot explain the reasons for some actions, we
are left to try to justify them. We should attempt to learn about other things that might be causing
people such behaviour and attempt to use these experiences in order to better control ourselves,
to help prevent future incidents of violence. Jonathan took this idea to heart. He set out to
achieve this. First, by following in his father’s footsteps and becoming a scholar. He studied

psychology, criminology and theology in an attempt to understand the meaning and source of
evil. While Jonathan was very interested in the subject, he soon realised that he had never really
understood it in all the study done and never would. The more time that passes and Jonathan
spends immersed in these studies he grows bored. There wasn’t anything special going on in his
life apart from school work and the occasional hangout with his friends. Jonathan has tried
everything he knows of to gain enlightenment of this mystery, with no answer in sight. And so
he decided to follow the path of a crime fighter Instead. Becoming a police officer. Jonathan is a
good cop, he does his job with passion and determination. It is during one of his patrols when the
attack happens. Jonathan was driving down the highway, having seen a gang of thugs beating
another guy for stealing a bag of chips from the gas station. Suddenly the van behind him crashes
through the guardrail into the middle lane and hits another vehicle full of civilians, including him
and his colleague. All five passengers and the driver are killed instantly, leaving Jonathan and his
partner uninjured except for minor cuts and bruises. The officer immediately radios for
emergency assistance. “You OK there partner?” The officer asked. Jonathan turns to face him.
“Yes I am.” But the driver of the van is not fine, still alive and in serious pain. Jonathan and his
partner walk over to check on him. When they get closer, they can smell the alcohol on him. The
man was clearly drunk and had just killed five people. Jonathan wonders why anybody could get
in front of the wheel knowing full well that they were intoxicated. ‘This is truly evil.’ Jonathan
thinks to himself. This was an accident but one that could have been easily avoided, why then
did it happen. The crux of the theory Jonathan’s dad had settled on was that these kinds of things
occurred at the hand of a malevolent force with forethought and intent. They had yet to figure out
more about this line of thinking and ideas.

Something appears at the corner of Jonathan’s eye, a shadow that moves towards him. He
sees what looks like a black van with white stripes across its sides idling next to the road. It was
definitely the same colour of van as the one that had just crashed. “A black van…with white
stripes…” Jonathan whispered to himself; he was confused. There was no way that this could be
a coincidence. The shadow then leaps at him, pushing him to the ground. He does not notice
what has just attacked him. His partner rushes towards him wondering why he is on the ground.
“What happened?” He asks. Suddenly, Jonathan’s partner goes flying through the air as if
something had pulled him up. He hits the ground hard, crashing his skull on the pavement.
Jonathan tries to fight off whatever is attacking them but he does not see it. He feels arms wrap
themselves tightly around his neck and tighten, choking him. Before his vision becomes fuzzy,
he hears a voice say, “I’ve got you now, little boy.” With the last of his strength, Jonathan
manages to break free. He stands back up and sees the van pull forward before disappearing
completely. Jonathan runs over to his partner and checks on him. “Are you okay man?” Jonathan
asked his partner who nods weakly. The van screeches back to where Jonathan was and its door
opens. A man wielding a strange weapon leaps out the van and appears to shoot at nothing. In the
distance there’s a shriek and then silence. “We got it sir.” Says the man with the weapon.
Jonathan looks over and in the van, he notices his father. His father’s expression tells him that he
understands everything that just happened. Jonathan’s eyes go wide at this revelation. Then
suddenly his father disappears in an explosion of flames, smoke, and debris. Jonathan passes out
from the Shock.
Jonathan spent the day in hospital. As for his partner, the doctors did all they could to
heal him, but he still succumbed to his injuries. Jonathan finally opens his eyes on Friday 12 pm,
after being unconscious for nearly 20 hours. He sits up on the bed and takes notice of the bright

orange sun shining through the windows of the room. His room was a mess, the sheets were
ripped and pillows were missing. He looks down to discover that he is still wearing his uniform.
It is a bit dirty, but otherwise fine. He then remembers the events from the day of the attack. His
eyes go wide once again. He jumps out of bed and makes his way towards the open window. Out
on the street he sees the black van parked a few feet ahead. Jonathan walks outside then up to the
van and puts his hand on top of the hood, leaning out. Inside he sees the driver. He seems to be
asleep. Jonathan grabs one of the broken bricks near him and swings his arm back hitting the
driver on the nose. The man wakes up immediately. He pulls out a gun, aimed directly at
Jonathan’s face. Jonathan quickly jumps out of sight before he gets shot, running along the
pavement of the hospital parking. Once Jonathan was clear of the building he stops running,
breathing heavily, taking deep breaths to try to calm his nerves. He hears a noise and sees a car
approaching rapidly from the street. The driver speeds up and speeds past him, almost clipping
his leg with the bumper. As Jonathan starts to run away again, he hears a voice coming from the
black van. “Hey you, stop, we’re not here to hurt you.” Not willing to take any chances, Jonathan
disappears into the traffic managing to evade the man in the van. The first place he goes to is his
apartment building to grab some things. He locks himself inside of the elevator and presses the
button marked 6. He hears the sound of the elevator starting up and heads to the third floor and
then to the sixth. At the end of the hallway is a large metal door with a small keypad next to it.
After pressing six keys, 626666, the door unlocks. Inside is exactly how Jonathan left it. The
only thing changed is that one of his pictures was taken down. Jonathan places his backpack on
the table and takes his laptop out. He searches for information on serial killer cases in the area
and finds that there has been an increasing number recently. There is one case in particular that
catches his attention. An eighteen-year-old girl, named Melissa Cooper, found dead two weeks

ago, stabbed multiple times with a blunt object. She lived in one of those houses that are located
further away from the main road. Apparently, she ran into trouble with her boyfriend and
attempted to run away with him. She was found dead in her house when nobody was home. The
police had ruled suicide as a possibility. DNA tests had not found any traces of alcohol or drugs
on her body. She had been stabbed seven times with a blunt instrument and her neck was also
fractured. One person was identified who worked for her boyfriend, an employee from the
grocery store on the block. This is how he would track his father down. He knew his father had a
keen interest in cases such as these and would be involved somehow. The second place he goes
to is the precinct. There, he would find case files for the Melissa murder case and maybe his
father would be mentioned as a consultant. He hoped so anyway.
Jonathan goes into the main office of the precinct and sees a veteran detective sitting at
the desk. “Hey Jon, need some help?” He asks. The detective looks surprised to see Jon.
Jonathan is always welcome at the precinct but it was technically forbidden to come in right after
his incident. “Um yeah, I’d like to request your help on a case. It may involve my father.”
Jonathan replies. “Oh yes sure! Come sit down.” The detective gestures to the empty seat next to
him. They both spend the next 15 minutes discussing the details of the case. Jonathan explains to
the detective that his father is part of an agency called the ‘Criminal Investigation Unit’ which
investigates serial killer cases. According to the records, his father works for one of the most
infamous law enforcement agencies in the world. Jonathan describes his situation to the detective
and explains why he needs a criminal expert’s perspective. The detective nods his head and says,
“It should not be too difficult. I have just started interviewing suspects on this case and should be
able to help you. We have a new suspect in custody so we have been assigned to him. If you
want, you can meet him whenever he is released. If you’re lucky he might talk to you but don’t

count on it. Now let us get to work.” Jonathan looks intently at the detective formulating an
appropriate response as to not arise suspicion. “Ah that’s OK I just need to know the location of
the temporary offices of the Criminal investigation unit. I have something I know they are going
to want to look at and I need to deliver it personally.” He says. The detective nods his head and
gives Jonathan the information for the new location. Jonathan thanks him and leaves the office.
He heads to the location hoping to find his father and get an explanation. He arrives and goes
upstairs, where the offices were. Once he reaches the top, he knocks on the doors. A female
officer answers the door after opening it a crack. “Can I help you?” Jonathan nods and holds up a
folder. “This is what I came for. I brought something for your team to review.” He explains.
“Thank you very much.” The woman responds. “Well do come on in then, I’m sorry I didn’t
recognize you earlier, I don’t get a lot of visitors here and you are the first one I have seen
today.” Jonathan looks surprised but follows her inside the room, closing the door behind him.
The area is surprisingly spacious considering the size of the offices. In front of them are the
desks where everyone normally works. There is also a long glass wall, probably to provide
access to the bullpen and the crime board. To the left of them are several cubicles. The desks
look identical to each other, the only difference is the name plate of the person at the nearest
desk. Next to the desk is a gentleman in his late thirties. He too has short light-coloured hair like
Jonathan’s. “Good afternoon officer Reed. How may I be of assistance today?” Asks the
gentleman. “I am looking for Dr Reed, my father and I need to talk to him right now please
where is he?” Unbeknownst to Jonathan, this place is not what it appears to be. The gentleman
tells Jon to sit down. “Your father will be with you right away; he has been expecting you. I’m
sure you two have a lot to catch up on.” Jonathan smiles politely and takes a seat on a nearby
chair. “Yes, thank you.” Then the door to the main office slides open revealing an elderly man

dressed in a suit and a tie. He approaches Jonathan’s desk and stands beside him. “Ah good,
Jonathan! Please join me, it looks like we’ll be having some serious conversation soon.” The
elderly man is Jon’s father Dr Reed. He leads Jonathan to his office in the corner. There is a brief
silence in the air between them before the elderly man breaks it. “So, Jonathan. You have come
here I suppose seeking answers.” Jonathan responds, “Sure.” His father tells him to go on.
“Where have you been this whole time and what the heck happened at the highway accident,
what attacked me and why?” A furious Jonathan asks. His father pauses for a second and
responds, “I know you are curious about what has happened and why I’m here. Allow me to
begin by telling you a little story. About 10 years ago I was assigned to an undercover mission in
London. I took care of a rather nasty man who was a major drug trafficker. What I saw there
changed the whole trajectory of my life. I chose to disappear from yours in order to protect you
from the world I had become entangled in.” Jonathan has a look of confusion on his face.
“You’re not making any sense dad.” Dr Reed looks within himself to find the best way to explain.
“There are evil things out there son. Things beyond this world. After learning about their
existence, I grappled with it for some years until I finally decided to do something about it. I
formed this organisation you are in right now. We are Defiance. The filter between this world
and the nexus.” He speaks. Jonathan looks at him with disbelief. It does not make any sense to
him. “What are you talking about?” He asks. Dr Reed continues. “I don’t have all the answers
yet, trust me I wish that I did. All I can tell you is, if the world gets out of control, I want the
people involved with it to be safe. That includes everyone who lives and breathes. But for me to
ensure this I needed to go underground. To remain hidden, I created Defiance. We were
supposed to operate in secret so that no one in this world knew who we really are. I wanted to
create a society in the middle of the chaos so that our kind could live peacefully without fear,

without being haunted and pursued, without being forced to commit heinous crimes. The first
thing I did was establish a special group called Deviants. I discovered that there was a group
called the Devil’s Den which was responsible for murdering innocent people and several other
crimes alike. The Deviants were selected to combat this threat. For the past 7 years they have
fought against the Devil’s den in the name of our community. I believed in them and I trusted
them. I made sure that their names were never associated with mine. The thing that attacked you
was a demon sent by the Devil’s den to get to me through you and the man who saved you, that
was one of my Deviants.” Jonathan sits there in total silence then suddenly he remembers; he
recognised the man that supposedly saved him. “Dad, that man..that man is a convicted murderer.
I recognise him from my work. I get that you are fighting demons or whatever but why use
criminals, let alone him? Are all your so-called deviants criminals? There have been a rush of
criminal escapes sweeping the prisons. Criminals worse of the worse escaping custody in broad
daylight. Why dad? Tell me you are not involved.” Jonathan screams at his dad in horror. “ It is
more complicated than that son, come with me and you will see.” Dr Reed walks over to the
window of his office and opens it slightly. “There is nothing more complicated than a criminal,
Jonathan. Just look outside and you will understand.” As Jonathan turns his gaze outside, he
notices his dad struggling to breathe. He chooses to not prod the old man about it. “What do you
mean dad?” He asks. Dr Reed stares out the window and laments. “Are criminals born or made?
The answer always seemed to be: a bit of both. The reality was, the causes of crime lie in a
combination of predisposing biological traits channeled by social circumstance into criminal
behaviour. Although I myself understood that the traits alone may not inevitably lead to crime
and that the circumstances do not make criminals of everyone. I held the position that put
together, these variables create a population responsible for a large fraction of the world’s

problem of crime in the streets. Well son, it turns out the was a third variable I overlooked…evil
itself. It exists and a select group of people wield it. In a world such as this, what is a sane man
like me supposed to do? Tell me.”

chapters.title: Dr Avery Melblac
chapters.text:
Five years ago, Avery was introduced to a self-styled Satanic high priestess who called
herself a witch. She dressed herself in flowing dark clothes and black eye shadow around to her
temples. The lady acknowledged worshiping Satan as her King. Avery was a woman of science
and a lover of history; after studying the classics at The Pearson institute, she trained in
psychiatry and in psychoanalysis at Columbia. Because of that background, a Catholic priest
from a small congregation had approached her for her professional opinion about whether this
woman was suffering from a mental disorder. This was at the height of a national panic about
Satanism. So she was inclined to scepticism. The woman’s behaviour exceeded what Avery
could explain with her training. The woman claimed she could tell some people their secret
weaknesses, such as a penchant for prostitutes as she claimed in one instance. She also claimed
to know how individuals she’d never known had died, and demonstrated this by revealing to
Avery how her father had passed from a fatal case of testicular cancer. Six people later vouched
to Avery that, during her exorcisms, they heard the woman speaking multiple languages,
including Latin, completely unfamiliar to her outside of her trances. This was not psychosis,
Avery thought to herself. She was apprehensive about it, but she deemed it was what she could
only describe as paranormal ability. She concluded that the woman was possessed. The priest
who had asked Avery of her opinion was the most experienced exorcist in this particular bizarre
case at the time. He was an erudite and sensible man. Avery had told him that, even as a
practicing Catholic, she was not likely to go in for a lot of the hocus-pocus. “Well,” He replied.
“Unless we believed you were not easily fooled, we would have hardly wanted you to help us.”

So began an unlikely partnership. For the next 2 and a half years and over several
consultations, Avery worked with the priest helping clergy from multiple denominations and
faiths to filter episodes of mental illness — which represent the overwhelming majority of cases
— from, literally what was the devil’s work. The same habits that shaped what she did as a
psychiatrist — open-mindedness, respect for evidence and compassion for suffering people —
led her to aid in the work of discerning attacks by what she believed were evil spirits and, just as
critically, differentiating these extremely rare events from medical conditions. Her expertise also
enabled her to conduct research into the psychological effects of possession, specifically the
impact on people’s emotions, the state of the mind and the spiritual realm. These discoveries
became part of the foundation upon which she constructed her own beliefs. It had been years
since Avery started working with the priest and she still knew very little about the man. The only
thing she knew was his name. Father Reed. He had told her that was his name. One day the priest
requested Avery accompany him to consult on a case involving a young boy who had killed his
parents and was being held at a juvenile facility. As they rode along the car park in their vehicle,
Avery noticed a tall man wearing a suit standing under a large tree watching them intently. The
man had a stern expression, like a man ready for business. Avery turned towards the priest,
“Who is he?” She asked while pointing at the man under the tree. The priest hesitated before
saying, “His name is Arthur.” Avery raised her eyebrows and asked again. “What does he do? Is
he an investigator too?” The priest shook his head while replying, “No, no. He’s a member of the
church. That’s all you need to know about him.” Avery stared at the priest for a few seconds
before asking again, “Why was he waiting for us under that tree?” She whispered. “He works for
the church.” The priest answered softly. Avery was intrigued and curious about this man. They
entered the juvenile facility. Once inside ,they went upstairs to the conference room where a

woman sat drinking tea. Before anyone could speak, the woman stood up. “Thank you for
coming Father Reed, it’s nice to finally meet you.” She smiled warmly to the priest and extended
her hand. “My name is Margaret.” A smile appeared on the priest’s face. “Margaret, thank you
so much for agreeing to see us.” The priest replies with a smile. Margaret looks at Avery
questioningly before asking the priest, “And who’s this young lady?” She questioned as she
gestured towards Avery. “Oh, this is Dr Avery, she is here to assess the boy.” He says in a strict
tone. Margaret’s smile disappears from her face immediately and she gasps quietly, staring wide
eyed and frightened. She stammers as she tries to find her words but fails. “Well….I don’t…I
don’t believe in devils or demons. But this…this I don’t understand.” She says to the priest.
“This young boy was a sweet boy, he always stayed out of trouble and then suddenly..well you
will see for yourself. Maybe you people have an explanation.” She adds. “You don’t believe in
them?” The priest asks as he points a finger towards Margaret. “Yes, that is true. I believe in God.
Not them, not devils, but God.” Margaret says firmly and then continues as though this
conversation does not exist. “We should begin.” She says. She then leads the priest and Avery
towards a single cell where the boy is being kept. The boy is lying down and asleep when they
enter the room. Avery then asks Margaret to leave them. Before she begins her usual routine in
these cases Avery muses, “Is it possible to be a sophisticated psychiatrist and believe that evil
spirits are, however rare, assailing humans? Most of my scientific colleagues and friends say no,
understandably so because of their frequent contact with patients who are deluded about demons
and their general scepticism of the supernatural. But careful observation of the evidence
presented to me in my career has led me to believe that certain extremely uncommon cases can
be explained no other way. Can this be one of such cases?” The priest tells her as he gestures
towards the boy. “This poor child has been infected by demonic forces through some ritual

performed by another member of this circle that I am sure you have heard of. It is said that when
this ritual is used and in its last phase, a demon enters a human body. Once possession takes
place, the person who performed the ritual gains an unnatural power from the victim. When this
happens to a person who is weak and afraid, the demon is able to gain great advantage over the
possessed person.” The priest explains. “How old is he?” Avery then inquires. “He is 16.” The
priest responds. “What did he do? And I see he has sustained some injuries; how did that
happen?” She goes on. “He murdered his parents. He claims not to remember the event or
anything after he left home. But according to his friends, he had blood all over his shirt and pants
and there are defensive scars on his chest.” The priest answers. After some questions and
discussion, they proceed to wake the boy. The boy opens his eyes and turns to look at them. His
eyes move from the priest to Avery and back again before opening his mouth slightly to form a
scream. Avery puts her index finger on her lips, whispering softly, “Shhhhh shhh…don’t scream,
shh shhhh,” as she reaches out towards the boy, trying to calm him, hoping his terror may
subside. But the boy screams louder. The priest looks at the boy angrily, and then shouts, “STOP
IT!” He grabs the boy by his shoulder and pulls him away forcefully causing the boy to fall off
the bed and onto the floor. The priest kneels next to the boy and gently strokes his forehead
while reassuring him. “Everything will be fine; nothing is going to hurt you.” In a matter of
minutes, the boy is sobbing uncontrollably, tears flowing profusely from his eyes and cheeks.
His teeth are chattering uncontrollably and his breathing becomes quick and shallow. The priest
then helps the boy get to his feet. “There now.” He says softly, placing his hand on the boy’s
back. The boy is shaking violently and crying and looking terrified. “Can someone please give
him something to calm down?” Asks the priest. He gestures to Margaret to bring something to
calm the boy down. Margaret runs out of the room and comes back moments later with a packet

full of pills. “Take these.” Margaret orders while handing the packet to the priest. The priest
hands the pills to the boy. “These will make you feel better and keep you safe.” He says. With
the pills taken, the boy seems to relax slightly. While they are both watching him, a man dressed
in a long black trench coat and a suit, black fedora hat and sunglasses walks into the building and
stops at the door. The priest nods to the officer who stands guard and walks towards the man
who is standing near the doors. They speak in hushed whispers for a few moments. Avery
notices it is the man from under the tree. The priest then returns to the room followed by the man.
“This man came here yesterday to take care of the boy.” The priest explains. “Did he do anything
strange to the boy?” Avery asked curiously. The priest pauses for a moment before continuing.
“Yes, I can’t recall the exact details but, I believe he placed an object under the boy’s
mattress…something like a dagger, a crucifix or something similar to ward off evil spirits. The
doctor here thinks the boy may have suffered from mental illness and may be suffering from
schizophrenia. However, we still haven’t determined the cause.”
“Do you think he might be one of these demonic creatures?” Avery asks again as she
continues to study the boy. Avery’s job was to determine if there was anything supernatural
involved or the mental state could explain his behaviour. On this day things were different. The
case was a lot more violent than the other previous cases she had consulted in. She was at a loss
but also determined to get to the bottom of it. As Avery was busy with the boy, the priest
appeared to be conspiring with the man he called Arthur. The two were hatching a scheme.
Unbeknownst to Avery, the priest and his men planned to take the boy out of the juvenile facility.
They had previously determined that the boy was being possessed by a demon that could be
turned. Almost every case of demon possession the priest and his cohorts had come across in the
past was vastly different. There were some demons that had personalities of their own and others

which did not. In the cases where they did not have personalities, the possessed individual would
be in a fugue state. They would sometimes have increased strength and stamina and at other
times they would merely be more cunning. The priest had discovered that in such cases, the
possessed individual could retain those abilities but be purified and rid of the demon itself. Some
characteristics of the demon would remain in the possessed individual and the individual would
retain control of their own mind. Reed found this very useful and often took advantage of it.
After turning the possessed he would then convert and recruit them to his cause. This boy was
merely the latest subject for his little experiment. Father Reed wanted to know why the boy was
acting so abnormally in the first place and what kind of demon controlled him. Arthur, who was
outside of the room, walked up to Father Reed and asked, "Have you decided yet? Do you want
to release him now or wait until morning?” When Avery heard this, she was taken aback. She
looked baffled and nervous at the same time.
“What are you going to do?” She asks loudly. Before the priest could give an answer, a
young officer comes running from outside. “They are here, you need to move quickly, the Den
has sent henchmen and they look really pissed off.” The young officer adds. Reed looks angry
and then turns towards Avery. “We will talk once this boy gets better.” He says as he prepares
the boy for travel. Arthur then pulls out a large weapon from his coat and begins to shoot. He
aims at the door where several men were attempting to enter with weapons of their own.
Margaret drops her tea and gets under the table, shaking from fear. The men exchange several
bullets with Arthur. Avery is now on the floor as well. She is scared. “Why the hell are they
shooting at us!?” She asks. “It appears our boy here may be too dangerous and they thought it
might be best to send somebody in to handle this.” Reed says in response while gesturing
towards the boy. “If you want to help the boy, we must get him out of here.” He adds. “No…it’s

too risky, I won’t risk putting him in danger. Besides, we have no proof of anything except
circumstantial evidence, we cannot afford to take this risk.” Avery counters. “But he needs
biblical attention.” The priest reasons. “It is not as bad as you seem to think. Look we’re driving
them back, we’re winning.” He grins oafishly. Suddenly the boy’s eyes turn bright red and he
begins foaming at the mouth. Avery and everyone else in the room begin trembling. “What do
we do!? What’s happening!?” Asks Avery. “The boy is transforming, there’s your evidence.”
Says Reed. “This is just the beginning!” He states before shouting to the henchmen outside.
“You guys let this boy go now and surrender yourselves.” The henchmen respond with more
gunfire. Reed then realizes that the boy was becoming bigger and bigger, his entire body
becoming covered in sharp horns and claws. Everyone around him began to tremble from fear
and panic. All except for Reed. A sinister smirk spreads across his face as he approaches the
transformed boy and begins speaking to him. “Come out child! Come out and play.” He says.
Avery looks at Arthur. “I don’t understand. Why would he lure him out?” She asked. Just at that
moment, the boy charges at them. The priest jumps back just before the beast could crash straight
into him. “Run! Run!” The creature begins hauling its body onto anything nearby. Avery cannot
believe her eyes. Margaret is just as shocked. When the creature launches itself onto the priest,
he begins to chant something in Latin: “Exorcizamus te, omnis immundus spiritus, omnis
satanica potestas, omnis incursio infernalis adversarii, omnis legio, omnis congregatio et secta
diabolica. Ergo, omnis legio diabolica, adiuramus te…cessa decipere humanas creaturas, eisque
æternæ perditionìs venenum propinare…Vade, satana, inventor et magister omnis fallaciæ, hostis
humanæ salutis…Humiliare sub potenti manu Dei; contremisce et effuge, invocato a nobis
sancto et terribili nomine…quem inferi tremunt…Ab insidiis diaboli, libera nos, Domine. Ut
Ecclesiam tuam secura tibi facias libertate servire, te rogamus, audi nos.” An air of silence

consumes the room and then suddenly the beast lets out an incongruous shriek that blasts out all
the surrounding glass. Everyone drops to the floor with their hands on their ears. The henchmen
were all scared by the encounter and hastily make their exist. Back inside the facility there’s a
black cloud hovering over the now passed out creature. It starts losing its demonic form
morphing slowly back into the young boy. “Oh my god.” Margaret breathes. “Its gone.” The
smoke clears up revealing the boy, still unconscious. Arthur enters the room, putting away his
gun. He walks over to the boy and examines him carefully. “Everything is alright down here.”
He says. He then looks at the boy and gives him an odd smile. “Yes, everything is fine.” Reed
says “Good because we’re ready to leave.” The next couple of hours passed with no sign of
either the boy or his captors. Reed tried contacting the local police force to find out if any of the
officers got hurt, but there was no reply. He was growing frustrated; he knew the police
department would not help them again in the future if his people did not effortlessly cover up this
job. Reed looked over at Avery who was staring out the window. He sighed deeply and walked
towards her. Avery turns to face the priest. She sees the look on his face. He looks tired, stressed
even. Reed sighs again and sits down beside Avery. “Well, that wasn’t exactly how I imagined
your first real Defiance mission to go…” He admits as he runs his fingers though his beard. His
mind reels as he thinks about what happened and how he should proceed from here. Avery
meanwhile keeps silent. She had no idea what to say to him, she had not really been expecting
the priest to have saved them so easily. However, she had been convinced that he would not be
able to resist giving his life for hers and Margaret’s. There wasn’t much doubt in Avery’s mind.
“Thank you for saving us but, I can assure you that I am done. I won’t go any further with you
unless you tell me the whole truth. Who are you really?” Reed looks at her intently, trying to
gauge her attitude towards the whole thing. Finally, he lets out a long sigh as he shakes his head.

“Well, I’m not really a priest. I’m a Dr just like you, the academic kind. I run a secret
organisation created Solely to root out evil. Those men back there, they are kind of like our
nemesis and they are really powerful but, so are we.” Avery does not look phased by this at all.
“Where do I sign up?”

chapters.title: Dr Andrew Reed
chapters.text:
When Dr Reed’s wife died, the days that followed her death were both utterly full and
completely empty. Full of activity yet empty of life. Much of the time he sleep walked through
the things he had to do. So numb that he was often completely unaware of what was going on
around him, cut off from everything that he thought was his life. Only a jarring event or a few
spoken words from his son would bring him out of his darkness. He often found himself
consumed by her death, becoming obsessed with the man that was responsible for her murder.
He wanted to kill him. He wanted to kill the man for taking away what little happiness he’d ever
known, and yet somehow, he could not put the blame on someone, even someone that he hated.
He spent many nights alone and miserable, waiting for some sort of distraction which never
came and instead only made him more depressed. Eventually, he did get some sort of comfort in
the form of his son. Jon Reed saw how miserable his father was and made efforts to put a smile
on his face. Bringing him his books and asking him if he would teach him a few things. Andrew
loved teaching his son and so his stupor slightly shifted each day. Him and Jonathan would
spend hours buried in text books of psychology. Specifically, topics on deviant behaviour. It was
as if they were both trying to understand the reason behind the actions of his wife’s murderer.
Their studies became his obsession, it gave him an outlet for his grief and sorrow.
Dr Reed eventually became a leading expert in criminal behaviour. He became a criminal
profiler. Often assisting the police in certain cases where they needed a detailed profile of the
unknown subject in an investigation. The criminal profiling process involved identifying the

perpetrator of a violent crime by identifying the personality and behavioural characteristics of the
offender based upon an analysis of the crime committed. As far back as the late 19th century,
investigators used psychology to understand and attempt to apprehend infamous killers. By
interviewing serial killers, terrorists and other violent criminals, Dr Reed was able to research the
psychopathologies and behavioural abnormalities of serious criminals, beginning the process of
identifying patterns and correlating behaviours. He was fascinated by analysing how criminals
think, react and behave according to specific variables within a given context, allowing him to
figure out the potential motives and motivations of offenders. He theorized that criminals are
often driven to commit the most violent acts due to a series of circumstances which trigger the
action. One famous theory of his was that the victim of a case will normally come to terms with
their own deaths when they discover the culprit of the crime. Thus, the motive becomes clear and
the crime becomes less violent. Dr Reed’s theories allowed him to work with police departments
and law enforcement to identify possible leads regarding their killer. In his research Dr Reed
came to a conclusion that the psychopathology of each criminal is a unique and complex subject
that is rarely studied nor understood. This made it difficult to understand why they act as they do.
Although a man of science, Dr Reed also contemplated the role of unknowable forces in the
fostering of bad men. When he was younger, his mother always warned of ancient evils lurking
in the dark. The woman was very convincing. Enough that he too believed in such things, but not
entirely. Even though his mother was dead, Dr Reed could still remember how she talked to him.
He could vividly recall those conversations, the ones about religion, philosophy and evil itself. It
was during the late 90’s when Dr Reed began to become intrigued by the religious beliefs
prevalent among criminals. During the same period, he discovered an article published on the
topic by an anthropologist named John H. Gass. The article described how criminals’ thoughts,

actions and behaviours tend to follow patterns based on various forms of worship. Gass claimed
that the pattern-less patterns of behaviour exhibited by criminals were a manifestation of the
belief system that they held in common with a group of deities. These gods were seen as
manifestations of the same personification of power, or deity that the criminals were worshiping.
This religion served as a catalyst for the criminality of the criminals themselves. Gass suggested
that in order to be considered true members of a group, you had to embrace a form of cult-like
thinking in order to fit in with it. It was in that article that Dr Reed finally connected the dots
with something that he had observed throughout his career.
One day an old friend of Dr Reed who was also an officer approached him with an
interesting case in London involving a ruthless drug trafficker. “I’m sorry to ask you of this old
friend but I need you to come to London with me! There’s a rather nasty bit of business I think
you should look at.” He says to him. “Of course, I’ll be there, when do we leave?” Asks the
doctor. “We leave in two days. I have to say, I didn’t think you’d be this eager. I appreciate it.”
Replies the officer with excitement shining in his eyes. The rest of Dr Reed’s weekend passed
with nothing else but packing and planning. Finally, the day came that they had to leave. Dr
Reed arrived at a private airport to board the plane to London. “Hi, I’m looking for Mr Tryon
Street.” He says to a clerk at the counter. ‘Oh, my word’ thinks the clerk ‘isn’t this a
coincidence?!’ “Just up those steps to your right sir, third door on the left.” Says the clerk. Dr
Reed thanks him and walks down the hall to room 306 and knocks on the door. The door opens
to reveal a tall well-built older man in a suit and tie. “Dr Reed!” The man exclaims. He then pulls
Dr Reed into an embrace “It’s great to see you! How are you doing?” Dr Reed looks happy to
see him. He looks at Tryon for a few moments before asking, “Not to sound rude or anything,
but where are the others?” Dr Reed did not think it was just going to be the two of them on such

an important mission. Tryon gestures outside the room and says. “They are waiting in the
conference room downstairs. Let me show you while I make sure the pilot has everything ready
for take-off.” Dr Reed makes his way to the conference room and inside he sees a team Tryon
had put together. It was made up of some of the most courageous and smart people he had ever
had the honour of working with. The team was made up of four men: David Tenebrae, Daniel
Sallies, Michael Smith, and Martin Creswell. They were a strange collection of individuals. All
of them had backgrounds that showed they had experienced quite a bit of trauma and hardship
over the years. Most of them were good looking older men, and there were also some very
dangerous individuals among them. David Tenebrae was a retired Navy SEAL. After serving his
country for 15 years he returned home to England as a civilian. He was originally supposed to
return to America but decided that living under false pretenses in another country wasn’t for him
anymore. He started travelling, doing odd jobs here and there, but now he worked as a mercenary
for hire. A large part of the money he earned went to cover his expenses and a small part of it
was used to pay for his travels. Sallies, was also a retired soldier. He is a former sniper in the
South African military and after retiring from service had gone into business running guns and
explosives. He was a brilliant marksman and had even trained Dr Reed himself. Creswell was the
head of security for Dr Reed’s university. He had graduated with a degree in sociology and
history. Now it seemed he would be joining in on helping him track down their suspect. These
men had been working alongside Dr Reed for many years now, they knew his habits and
behaviours better than anyone. They had all previously assisted in investigations and taken risks
on Dr Reed’s behalf, and it had proven beneficial. He trusted them with his life and they had
proven themselves to be a loyal and trustworthy team. The meeting goes smoothly.

David Tenebrae and Daniel Sallies were familiar with the case from the start and knew
the routine procedure. Creswell and Smith had never done any of the actual investigating on the
case as they simply acted as backup for David and Sallies. Dr Reed sat silently watching them all
discuss the case, occasionally interjecting with questions and opinions here and there. After
discussing the case, Tryon Street returns to the conference room informing them that the plane
was ready to take off. After the initial preparations are made, they all board the private jet Tryon
had organised for them. As everyone is getting situated in the plane, Tryon asks everyone to take
a seat. They begin to discuss their plans for the coming weeks, giving Dr Reed enough time to
finish preparing. Dr Reed had already decided what he wanted to do and he planned on
researching it extensively before leaving the house that night. They discussed the various
scenarios, which ranged from finding out what was happening, to getting the suspect alive.
However, there was one scenario that worried them the most. That the suspect would find out
that they were going to investigate him and would retaliate against them once he found out. The
team agreed to ensure that fact be kept secret until they got him dead to rights. Every man on that
plane was ready to face any danger as it came, but one thing was for sure, they were all worried
about Dr Reed. They were not sure that Dr Reed was capable of handling the situation. They had
agreed to keep him away from the action for a short period of time so that they could observe
him. Once they were satisfied with his performance, they would allow him to continue to
participate in the operation. After making sure that everyone knows their roles the pilots begin
taking off.
It was mid-August 18th in London and the sun was bright enough for it to be considered
pleasant. The air conditioning at the hotel was running full blast. While the air conditioner was
currently keeping people calm and cool, no one seemed to want to move. The group was

gathered around the apartment room table discussing their plans for the next week. There were
three men sitting around the table. One was Dr Reed and the other two were two of his
colleagues, Daniel Sallies and David Tenebrae. Their main task for the day was to go into a
house on the other side of town, find evidence of a drug trade operation and determine any of the
suspects who may have been involved in the smuggling ring. The house was located deep within
an abandoned industrial sector, meaning that it took them a couple of hours to travel there
without incident. Unfortunately for Dr Reed, he found the journey much longer than he expected.
It took them over 3 hours to reach the location. The road they travelled was mostly silent and
there was hardly any other traffic. The area in which the house was located was relatively
deserted, although it was still early in the morning and the streets were fairly empty. They parked
in front of the house and then walked towards the building. Inside there was a long hallway,
presumably leading to the living quarters and bedroom area. When they entered the apartment
and began to look around, it became clear that the place was not completely abandoned. They
were forced to wait until it was dark to search the place. In order to make sure they would not
miss anything, Daniel Sallies set out an infrared flashlight that he carried with him everywhere
and he turned it on and pointed it down the hallway. On one side there were some shelves filled
with books. There was no sign of any illegal activities that they could detect so far. There was no
sign of any drugs or weapons either. Dr Reed noticed a box in the corner of the room covered
with dust. He walked over and uncovered the box revealing what appeared to be a stack of files,
each labelled with a date. He pulled out the top file, opened it and read through the pages. His
expression changed from one of surprise to excitement at what he found. He stood up and turned
toward the others in the room to let them know what he was about to tell them. He looked up
from the file briefly, and said, “I think we have just received a new lead.” They waited eagerly

for him to continue with what he had to tell them, but he stayed quiet for a moment before
continuing. “What I have discovered in here seems to confirm a belief I had before, namely that
our man is a professional hacker and hacker extraordinaire. These files contain information
regarding his work. From these files I can extrapolate data like from where he learnt how to hack
computers to what he has been able to accomplish by using advanced technology.” The others
looked at each other in amazement. It sounded like a huge break. Dr Reed continued. “In
addition to that, there are pictures of strange markings and what looks a lot like religious
paraphernalia” “What can you tell us from that Dr?” Asks one of the men in the room. Dr Reeds
looks at them intently and responds. “I have no idea.”
It is getting late so they all agree to wrap it up and meet with the others back at the hotel.
Tyron street had taken the rest of the team on a separate mission and they too were about to wrap
up on their end. The team cleaned up evidence they were there and headed back to the hotel.
Once there, Dr Reed went into his room and started rummaging through the contents of his
suitcase. He came up with a laptop and placed it on the desk and opened up the internet
connection. He searched a site and brought up images of similar symbols he had found back at
the scene on his screen. He was trying to find something similar but it didn’t seem to work. Dr
Reed could not help but feel that someone was playing with him. He couldn’t quite place it but
he was sure he had seen these symbols before. After pondering this thought he began to write up
a report, detailing his findings. Just as he is doing that, Tryon and his team return from their
mission. The men all gather in the same room to discuss everything they had come to learn on
this day. Once everyone is present, Dr Reed continues writing his report for the benefit of the
team. When Dr Reed finishes up, he hands his report over to Tryon and says, “You guys have
been amazing. I wish my reports were half as good as yours.” Tryon smiles at him while saying,

“Thank you, but I think your reports are outstanding. I think you should be given more
recognition.” He looks at the team and continues. “Ok gentlemen, so what did you learn.” The
team then recounts their experiences on this day. They leave out few details. When Dr Reed
speaks, he tells them his report is incomplete. “I found multiple images of strange symbols at the
scene, I’m not certain what they are yet but I know they are significant.” He says. All of them
seem interested in his report and he continues with his findings. He tells them, “I am looking for
patterns, for signs of an organized criminal organisation.” The team all share a collective smile
of joy as they realise what Dr Reed is hinting at. “We’ll be able to track these mysterious
symbols, if they are linked to the gang we’re after.” He tells them. They all nod and smile
happily at Dr Reed. At that moment he could tell that there was something they were keeping
from him. He decided to keep these suspicions to himself and proceeded with his presentation.
After about an hour of this, everyone was ready to retire to bed for the night. Dr Reed decided to
stay up and decode those strange symbols. He was working his way through page after page
trying to understand the meanings behind the symbols he saw. By now it was almost midnight
and Dr Reed had been working steadily for several hours. Eventually, the doctor gave up,
deciding to call it a night and get some sleep. He had a very big day tomorrow and he knew he
needed to be well rested if he was to function properly. As he drifted off to sleep a sudden rush
of memories flooded his mind. He recalled the day of his wife’s murder and the man who was
responsible. He remembered that the man was covered in tattoos all over his body and face. One
of the tattoos on the man resembled the symbols he discovered at the scene. This jarred him
awake and he frantically went to his bag and looked at the file with the symbols on it. “This is
it.” He says to himself. He had a look of disbelief in his eyes. He went over to check on his
teammates and saw that they were all fast asleep. Dr Reed then did the unthinkable. He went

outside, got in their car and drove back to the house they were at earlier that day. Alone. When
he arrived, the house was still empty. He made his way inside. He closed and locked the door
behind him. As he walked through the house, he examined every inch of the place trying to find
a clue to the identity of their mystery man he may have missed on their first sweep. When he
reached the bedroom Dr Reed sat down on the floor with his hands on his head. Memories of his
wife broke him to pieces. Sitting there, he saw that one of the drawers in the closet had fallen
open. The bottom drawer was pushed all the way out of the space causing it to swing open and
reveal what was underneath it. Dr Reed reached inside the drawer and pulled out a large brown
envelope. Inside the envelope was a piece of paper with handwritten words scribbled on it in
black ink. He picked it up and unfolded it. He held the paper carefully so that only the first words
of it were visible. “Beneath the mask you wear.” Dr Reed read the words slowly and carefully,
attempting to memorise the words. He thinks to himself, “how did I miss this,” while starring at
the text. He stared at it for nearly 20 minutes thinking about the message, trying to puzzle out its
meaning. He is yanked out of this daze by a sound of raffling coming from inside the room. He
thought he was alone but maybe not. Then a man appears and knocks him out.
Dr Reed wakes up in a panic. He immediately realises he does not know where he is, and
when he looked around, he found himself surrounded by darkness. The room was cold, the only
source of light being the moonlight shining through the windows, illuminating the room slightly.
As Dr Reed took deep breaths in an attempt to calm down, he also realized that he was not alone
anymore. He could hear voices. He tried desperately to discern where the voices came from.
When he managed to catch sight of the silhouettes of the other three people in the room, he
recognised one of them instantly. The man who had knocked him out earlier was sitting on the
couch. The second figure was also on the couch. The third figure was standing in front of the

door near Dr Reed and he could see clearly that the stranger was holding a knife. Dr Reed knew
that he had to be careful or else the stranger would attack him. He slowly sat up and moved
backwards on the bed, keeping the man in his line of vision. The stranger stepped closer to him,
his movements slow and deliberate. He pointed the knife directly at Dr Reed. A small smile
appeared across the stranger’s lips as he raised his knife. In that instant Dr Reed understood why
the man was smiling. He was going to cut him, slice away at him with the knife in his hand.
Suddenly the stranger froze and then turned towards the doorway. There stood the other two men.
“Who the hell is this guy?” One of the men asks. His partner looks at him and says he does not
know and that he found Dr Reed at the hideout. They bicker back and forth about what they
should do with him. One of them suggests killing him. The other agrees and walks towards Dr
Reed. “Get away from me!” Dr Reed shouts. Both of them stop and turn around looking angry.
“Why don’t you just leave me the fuck alone!” Dr Reed yells. The stranger steps closer and grabs
hold of the back of Dr Reed’s neck pulling him close. He then stabs him in the stomach. A gasp
leaves Dr Reed’s mouth as blood begins to drip onto the bedspread. “Wait don’t kill him. We
could use him for the ritual. Killing him would be a waste.” Says one of the men. Dr Reed lays
on the bed, bleeding. He was alive for now. The stab was not fatal. Hours pass and he wonders if
his friends were even looking for him. The men that took him finally return to the room, this time
they have a young woman with them and a collection of odd objects. They hurl him out of the
bed and place him in the middle of the room on top of a strange symbol curved on the floor. The
woman takes out a vial of blood from a satchel she is carrying and drips it on Dr Reed’s forehead
before reciting a prayer in Latin. “Daemon, esto subjecto voluntati meae!” She repeats this
several times. As she recites the incantation, the man who knocked Dr Reed out earlier kneels
next to her, holding a small dagger in his hands. “I am the god who sends his gifts, I am the lord

who makes the world right.” After reciting the phrase over and over again the man puts the
dagger into Dr Reed’s chest, twisting it around until it reaches the heart. The woman chants once
more “I am the god who grants the gift of life.” Dr Reed feels the dagger enter his body but
nothing changes. He tries to say something but no noise comes out of his mouth. He tries to
reach for the dagger, wanting to stop it but the pain in his stomach and chest only seems to grow
worse. He feels his lungs begin to burn, he struggles against the dagger but, it has already pierced
his heart. The last thing he sees before his vision goes black is the man smiling at him while
whispering one word in Spanish, “Santo.” Before the group could finish the ritual, they were
performing on Dr Reed, his team blast through the door. They begin shooting, hitting one guy on
the head and the woman on her upper body several times. In the end, the room is left bloody with
the group holding Dr Reed all dead on the floor. “God what do we do.” Tryon asks worriedly as
he rushes over to Dr Reed’s lifeless body. Martin looks at the little book the woman was using
for her incantation. He analysis the page and figures out that they could finish the ritual with a
slight tweak and Dr Reed could be revived. “Here hold this.” He says while handing Daniel his
gun. He kneels down on the floor next to Reed and begins to recite a Latin incantation reading
from the book. The rest of the team are not shocked by his immediate response. It was not
Martin’s first time doing this, he knew exactly what he was doing. After several minutes of this,
Dr Reed gasps and suddenly opens his eyes with a look of bewilderment in his eyes.

chapters.title: Miles Kelly
chapters.text:
It has been two nights since Miles was taken from prison. He hasn’t gotten a clue where
he is or why he was taken. All he knows thus far is he was taken by some group calling
themselves Defiance. Miles has tried to escape but has been easily beaten by the men who guard
this place. The same men who attacked him during their raid at the prison. For these two nights,
Miles slept in a dark, damp and filthy cell. Every now and then a guard walked in with food
which he put into the small hole in the wall at the front of the cell. During each meal, a guard
would walk in with a bucket filled with water and give the captives one drink. At first it was
gross and disgusting, but after the third night it was less than horrible. On the fourth morning
someone entered the cell and began throwing some kind of metal chain through the hole in the
wall and attached the chains to Miles. The man unlocked the door allowing Miles to eat a couple
of soggy crackers that he placed in the cell for him. Miles felt like crap but he ate everything and
drank the entire bucket. When he finished the bucket, he was handed a new one and drank that
too. The next day another tray of food was brought to him along with the same bucket full of
water and the same set of handcuffs. By this point Miles was losing his mind. Each day there
would be one less person in that damned place. Miles noticed that they would remove at least
one person a day from the cells and that person would never return. He had his theories on what
they were doing to them. Miles figured that they must be torturing them. Every day he asked one
of the guards why they did not just kill him. None of the guards seemed to have an answer for
him. He made friends with the girl in the cell next to him. Her name was Sarah. Her hair was
short and black. She has beautiful dark skin. Although she was a bit hesitant at first, she

eventually got comfortable talking to Miles. Together they would share stories through the walls
on the lives they wished they had. They both dreamt of escaping. Miles never revealed to her that
he was a convicted murderer and that he was kidnapped straight out of prison. He did not realise
that she was also taken from a women’s prison where she was serving time for numerous
aggregated assault charges. It was not apparent to Miles yet, but each person taken by Defiance
had been a resident in a prison. “Hey Sarah, is it me or was the food so much better today?” Says
Miles to Sarah. Sarah let’s out a weak smile and admits that the food has been getting a lot better.
“What do you think happens to everyone they take away?” She asks Miles. “Maybe they kill
them.” replies Miles, a sad and frightened expression appearing on his face. He thought it
sounded unlikely because he was not sure if they actually killed anyone. Sarah gives a faint
laugh and says she thinks it is probably more likely they make them work for them until they die.
Miles laughs at this and says, “That sounds pretty bad.” She agrees. They both reassure one
another that they are lucky to be alive. Neither one believes it, but it does not hurt to humour
each other. After they talk, they both fall asleep side by side in the dark and cold cell. The next
evening Miles woke up from a nightmare in which his mother was standing behind him, telling
him that he could not do any good here and that he needed to return home. When Miles woke up,
he looked at Sarah lying asleep near him in the next cell. He decided to get up and try to find a
way out of his current predicament. He quietly climbed off of the cot and quietly tiptoed over to
the bars of the window. He looked outside, trying to work out ways to escape. He did this for the
rest of the day. The next morning, he could hear foot stamping coming from outside the cells. It
was the guards, and this time there were a lot more of them. They entered the cells and started
removing the remaining people out of their cells. It appeared they were taking them all at once
instead of just one at a time. “Miles!” Sarah screams for him as they take her away. “Hey what

are you doing to her.” He says to the guards. They carry him out and begin escorting everyone
else out as well. Outside the cell area is a long white corridor. They walk inside. There are a
number of rooms and a large door leading to a huge hallway. They march him and the rest of the
captives towards the large double doors and open them to reveal a large crowd of men and
women wearing white lab coats. Some of them were wearing hooded cloaks. There have many
different kinds of weapons which they held at arm's length. A guard pushes Miles forward and he
slowly walks in with the rest of the guards following him. They push the captives further in and
line them all up in front of an enormous wooden table standing with their backs facing it. One of
the captives drops to the floor and lets out a shrill scream. He was crying and screaming for help,
begging for someone to rescue him. Another male figure takes out a syringe and places it into the
neck of this young man who was kneeling and sobbing on the ground. He had been bleeding
from a self-inflicted wound on his wrists. Miles tries to step forward to assist him but one of the
guards grips his shoulder tightly forcing him to stay still. After a few seconds of bleeding, it was
too late for him to be saved and he falls onto his side. He was dead. Miles starts shaking in fear
at this sight. Men in cloaks then take the body away, leaving the rest of the captives stunned and
afraid. A guard pulls out a pistol and points it at Miles. “We need you to do us a favour.” He says
turning to look at Miles. “Call it fourth.” He says to Miles. Miles had no clue what the man was
taking about and so he begins to shiver. Miles asks, “What are you talking about.” The guard
looks at his boss, seeking approval. Miles looks over at Sarah who does not appear fazed at all.
One of the lab coats then gestures at her. “Try her first.” He instructs. Miles feels sick with anger
and fear when the guard grabs Sarah by the arm and lifts her up to stand next to him. She stands
silently next to Miles and stares at the scene in front of them, unable to tear her gaze from the
pool of blood in front of her. The guard places the muzzle of the gun right between Sarah’s eyes.

She flinches slightly but does not say anything, waiting for what should happen next. The guard
fires the gun next to her ear, appearing to shoot her. She screams out in pain. She falls forwards
into Miles’ arms. He holds her close to him not knowing what to do with her. Suddenly Miles
starts laughing maniacally, frightening Sarah. She begins to feel a strange heat emanating from
his body. Avery Melblac looks at her colleagues and says, “I knew he had one in him; I saw it in
his eyes.” Sarah then pulls away from Miles who is still laughing and getting hotter and hotter.
Avery grabs a syringe with sedatives in it and shoots up Miles and he falls to the ground.
Hours pass by. Miles finally wakes up. He finds himself sitting in a chair inside a white
